title: DevOps
text: DevOps Cloud, Kubernetes, CI/CD, automation, infrastructure.

title: Kubernetes Backup and Restore Operations for GitOps Workloads
headingPath: Kubernetes Backup and Restore Operations for GitOps Workloads
text: Kubernetes Backup and Restore Operations for GitOps Workloads Summary Open with the practical thesis: a Kubernetes restore plan is only credible when it covers desired state, cluster API objects, persistent volume data, application level state, secrets, and control plane state separately. Argo CD uses Git repositories as the desired state source and continuously compares live state against that target, while Velero backs up Kubernetes objects and persistent volumes and etcd snapshot restore is a separate control plane recovery path [18][10][9]. Do not let the page imply that any single tool is enough to recover a production service. 18 10 Frame the operator's job as matching recovery mechanisms to failure modes and business objectives. RTO is the maximum acceptable outage duration and RPO is the maximum acceptable age of recoverable data, so backup schedules, storage location durability, restore automation, and drill cadence should be chosen to meet those targets rather than copied from a generic best practice checklist [21]. NIST CSF 2.0 treats recovery as restoration of affected assets and operations, which supports writing this page as an operational readiness guide, not merely a backup installation note [22]. 18 10 State the default architecture: GitOps for desired state, Velero for Kubernetes objects and volumes, application native procedures for databases, and etcd snapshots for the control plane [18][10][9]. 18 10 Make the reader choose explicit RPO/RTO examples such as "configuration restore within 30 minutes" or "database data loss below 15 minutes" before selecting backup frequency or tooling [21]. 18 10 Treat every restore as a production change with a rollback decision, target namespace or cluster, and validation criteria before touching live traffic [10][16]. 18 10 Decision Matrix Build the matrix around recovery surfaces. For bad manifests or accidental object deletion, start with GitOps reconciliation and Velero object restore; for persistent volume rollback, prefer CSI snapshots when the storage driver supports Kubernetes VolumeSnapshot APIs; for long term or cross provider durability, add CSI Snapshot Data Movement; for unsupported snapshot backends, consider Velero File System Backup with its beta, live filesystem, and privilege caveats; for database consistency, use application native dump, WAL, or quiesce flows alongside Velero hooks [8][11][12][13][15]. 8 11 Make the tradeoffs explicit instead of presenting a single recommendation. Kubernetes VolumeSnapshot is a point in time request that depends on CSI support, while File System Backup can cover many volume types but reads live file systems and is less point in time consistent than snapshot approaches [1][13]. For zone constrained storage, StorageClass volumeBindingMode matters because Immediate binding can provision before Pod scheduling constraints are known, whereas WaitForFirstConsumer delays binding until the workload exists [3]. 8 11 Use GitOps and kubectl apply/diff when the desired state in Git is correct and the failure is drift or accidental Kubernetes object deletion [8][18]. 8 11 Use Velero CSI snapshot backup for PVC backed data when the CSI driver and VolumeSnapshotClass are known to work in the target cluster [1][11]. 8 11 Use CSI Snapshot Data Movement when storage snapshots are not durable enough, must survive storage system failure, or need to move across providers [12]. 8 11 Use File System Backup only with a documented acceptance of beta status, live filesystem consistency, hostPath limitations, and node agent security implications [13]. 8 11 Use etcd snapshot restore only for control plane disaster recovery, and treat it as creation of a new logical cluster, not a live object level rollback [9]. 8 11 Reference Architecture Describe the architecture as six layers. Layer 1 is Git, where Kubernetes manifests, Helm values, Kustomize overlays, and Argo CD application definitions live. Layer 2 is Kubernetes API object backup, where Velero captures API resources into object storage. Layer 3 is PV data protection, using CSI snapshots, CSI snapshot data movement, or File System Backup depending on storage support. Layer 4 is application consistency, where backup hooks or database native dumps flush or freeze state before a snapshot. Layer 5 is secrets and encryption, where API resource encryption and RBAC are prerequisites for safely preserving sensitive objects. Layer 6 is control plane etcd snapshots for cluster state disasters [18][10][11][12][15][6][7][9]. 18 10 Show the data flow in prose: scheduled Velero backups create timestamped recovery points, write Kubernetes object archives to object storage, and snapshot or copy volume data when configured [10][17]. During restore, the backup storage location should be treated as the source of truth and can be set read only to avoid accidental creation or deletion while recovery is underway [10]. The target restore should begin in an isolated namespace or clean cluster unless the incident requires an explicitly approved in place update [16]. 18 10 Keep Git repositories independently protected; GitOps cannot restore a cluster if the desired state repository, secrets workflow, or deployment keys are unavailable [18]. 18 10 Keep object storage for Velero backups separate from the failed cluster and restrict mutation during restore windows [10]. 18 10 Document each StorageClass reclaimPolicy and volumeBindingMode, because deletion defaults and topology binding affect whether a restored workload can attach storage [2][3]. 18 10 Include an etcd snapshot path only for control plane rebuild scenarios, with revision bumping guidance for Kubernetes consumers [9]. 18 10 Restore Runbook Start the runbook with triage, not commands. Identify the failure type, decide whether the target is a new cluster, a new namespace, or an in place repair, freeze unrelated deploys, and preserve evidence such as backup names, namespace names, PVC names, StorageClass details, and the desired RPO/RTO. Velero restores are non destructive by default and skip existing resources unless an update policy is selected, so the safest default drill is a clean namespace or clean cluster restore with namespace mapping [16][10]. 16 10 Then provide command templates with warnings. Supported examples include velero restore create <RESTORE NAME from backup <BACKUP NAME namespace mappings old ns:new ns , velero restore create <RESTORE NAME from backup <BACKUP NAME parallel files download <NUM wait for fs restore or CSI snapshot data movement cases, and kubectl diff f <directory followed by kubectl apply f <directory for declarative object reconstruction [16][8]. For etcd, show only a fenced, clearly labeled control plane recovery template such as etcdctl snapshot save snapshot.db and etcdutl snapshot restore snapshot.db bump revision <N mark compacted data dir <output dir ; note that this starts a new logical cluster and must not be treated as a live namespace rollback [9]. 16 10 Preflight: record backup name, target cluster, target namespace, affected PVCs, StorageClass, VolumeSnapshotClass, application version, and traffic cutover condition [16]. 16 10 Restore safely first: use namespace mapping into a non serving namespace where possible, then validate pods, PVC binding, service discovery, and application integrity before exposing traffic [16]. 16 10 For Argo CD state, use the documented argocd admin export and argocd admin import path, and include the namespace flag when Argo CD is not installed in the default namespace [19]. 16 10 For declarative manifests, use kubectl diff before kubectl apply , and avoid mixing apply with create or replace management methods [8]. 16 10 For etcd restore, require a separate control plane change ticket and operator pair review because snapshot restore creates a new logical cluster and changes member identities [9]. 16 10 Failure Scenarios Cover common failure scenarios with the first safe move, the restore method, and the validation test. For accidental manifest deletion or drift, use GitOps reconciliation or declarative apply, then check that live state matches the desired commit [18][8]. For namespace level deletion, use Velero resource filters or namespace mapping to restore into a controlled target and validate CRDs, RBAC, Services, and PVC binding before exposing clients [14][16]. For a corrupted PVC, choose CSI snapshot restore or data movement if supported; otherwise consider FSB or application native restore with the documented consistency limitations [1][11][12][13]. 18 8 Include less obvious failures that create false confidence. If a database accepted writes during a volume snapshot and no application hook or logical backup was used, the storage image may not meet the application's consistency expectations even if the volume attaches successfully [15][10]. If a cluster restore targets a newer or different Kubernetes API surface, Velero requires the backed up API group/version to exist in the target cluster, so restore tests must include CRDs and operators, not only Deployments and Services [10]. If the incident is control plane loss, etcd disaster recovery is a separate path that uses a snapshot database to form a new logical cluster [9]. 18 8 Bad deploy or config drift: revert Git commit or apply known good manifests, then allow Argo CD to sync or use kubectl apply with diff preview [18][8]. 18 8 Deleted namespace: restore with namespace mappings into a quarantine namespace first, then validate application and data before traffic cutover [16]. 18 8 PV corruption: use CSI snapshot or data mover when available; use FSB only after accepting beta status and live filesystem limitations [11][12][13]. 18 8 Secret exposure: rotate credentials after restore and verify encryption at rest and RBAC before treating restored Secrets as safe [6][7]. 18 8 Control plane failure: follow etcd snapshot restore, revision bump, and compaction guidance; do not attempt to solve it with namespace level Velero restore alone [9]. 18 8 Operational Checklist Write this as a checklist that an on call or platform owner can run monthly and before risky migrations. Confirm that every production namespace has a declared recovery owner, RPO, RTO, backup schedule, retention policy, restore target, and validation test. Check that Velero scheduled backups are producing timestamped recovery points, TTLs match retention policy, and filters are scoped to the intended namespaces and resource kinds [17][14][10]. 17 14 Add storage and workload checks that prevent surprises. Inventory StorageClasses for reclaimPolicy, volumeBindingMode, expansion settings, and snapshot support; ensure StatefulSet volume retention is understood; verify that no persistent application data depends on Docker writable layers or Kubernetes ephemeral assumptions; and confirm that backup storage access is separated from normal cluster credentials [3][4][20][13]. Kubernetes CronJobs can run operational jobs, but missed schedule behavior and controller cadence mean successful backup completion needs monitoring rather than blind trust in cron syntax [5]. 17 14 Daily or per shift: check latest backup completion, age, size trend, and error count; alert when newest backup age exceeds the declared RPO [21]. 17 14 Weekly: perform at least one namespace mapped restore of a representative workload and record elapsed restore time against RTO [16][21]. 17 14 Monthly: restore a PVC backed StatefulSet and verify application level data, not only Pod readiness [4][11][13]. 17 14 Quarterly: run a control plane tabletop covering etcd snapshot location, restore commands, revision bump decision, and certificate or kubeconfig dependencies [9]. 17 14 After every storage driver or CSI driver change: re test VolumeSnapshotClass behavior and Velero restore into a non serving namespace [1][11][3]. 17 14 Common Pitfalls Lead with false assumptions. A StatefulSet keeps sticky Pod identity and stable storage, and Kubernetes intentionally avoids deleting associated volumes when scaling down or deleting the StatefulSet, but that is a data safety behavior, not a backup strategy [4]. Docker's writable container layer is also not a persistence layer: Docker documents that it does not persist after container deletion and is a poor choice for write intensive data, so the page should tell readers to use volumes or Kubernetes PVs for data that matters [20]. 4 20 Then cover restore specific traps. Velero backups are not strictly atomic, so highly active object changes can fall outside a backup [10]. FSB can back up many volume types but is beta quality, less consistent than snapshot approaches, and may require privileged node agent behavior in some environments [13]. Secrets are stored unencrypted in etcd by default, which means backup archives containing Secrets require encryption, least privilege access, and rotation procedures after exposure [6][7]. 4 20 Do not assume "pod is Ready" means "data restored correctly"; require application specific integrity checks [10][15]. 4 20 Do not enable broad restore filters in production without a target namespace or clean cluster plan [14][16]. 4 20 Do not keep only storage system snapshots when the storage system itself is in the failure domain; use data movement or object storage where durability requirements demand it [12]. 4 20 Do not rely on CronJob alone for backup assurance; monitor missed runs and backup freshness against RPO [5][21]. 4 20 Do not restore etcd snapshots casually; official guidance treats restore as a new logical cluster and recommends revision bumping for Kubernetes contexts [9]. 4 20 Maintenance Notes Make maintenance concrete. Update the page whenever Kubernetes, Velero, Argo CD, CSI drivers, StorageClasses, VolumeSnapshotClasses, backup bucket policy, encryption configuration, or database engine versions change. Velero restore depends on backed up API group/version availability in the target cluster, so CRD and operator upgrade plans must include restore validation, not just forward migration testing [10]. StorageClass changes need backup review because reclaimPolicy, volumeBindingMode, expansion behavior, and mount options directly affect restore outcomes [3]. 10 3 Define a practical cadence: daily backup freshness checks, weekly representative restores, monthly stateful workload restores, quarterly etcd disaster recovery tabletop, and pre migration restore drills before Kubernetes or storage upgrades. Use the NIST and DR planning framing to keep the focus on readiness: recovery actions should be ready before an incident, and RTO/RPO results from drills should feed improvements to schedules, automation, and documentation [22][21]. 10 3 Maintain a compatibility table for Kubernetes version, Velero version, CSI driver, VolumeSnapshotClass, StorageClass, object storage, and encryption settings [10][11][1][3]. 10 3 Record the last successful restore date and elapsed restore time for each critical workload, not merely the last successful backup time [21]. 10 3 Review Velero backup TTL and schedule owner reference choices so GitOps tooling does not prune or fight day two Backup and Restore resources unexpectedly [17]. 10 3 Review Secrets handling after every key management or RBAC change, and rotate restored credentials after suspected exposure [6][7]. 10 3 Keep Argo CD export/import commands pinned to the running version and namespace used in the cluster [19]. 10 3 Sources 1. kubernetes.io 2. kubernetes.io 3. kubernetes.io 4. kubernetes.io 5. kubernetes.io 6. kubernetes.io 7. kubernetes.io 8. kubernetes.io 9. etcd.io 10. velero.io 11. velero.io 12. velero.io 13. velero.io 14. velero.io 15. velero.io 16. velero.io 17. velero.io 18. argo cd.readthedocs.io 19. argo cd.readthedocs.io 20. docs.docker.com 21. docs.cloud.google.com 22. nvlpubs.nist.gov

title: Kubernetes Backup and Restore Operations
text: Kubernetes Backup and Restore Operations Summary This page treats Kubernetes recovery as a three layer problem: control plane state, declared desired state, and runtime/application data. Kubernetes stores API objects in etcd, so etcd snapshots are the emergency path for a lost control plane, while GitOps repositories and Velero backups cover the declarative and cluster resource layers [1][2][11][18]. PersistentVolume data needs separate treatment because etcd stores Kubernetes object metadata, not an application consistent copy of every backing disk or database file [4][5]. 1 2 The operating assumptions are an etcd backed Kubernetes cluster, workloads split between stateless Deployments and PersistentVolume backed StatefulSets, a separate object store or backup target, and operators who can perform an isolated restore drill without touching production. RTO and RPO should be written as measurable service objectives rather than informal labels: for example, a platform tier may accept a 60 minute RPO and four hour RTO for internal tools, while a customer facing data service may need a 15 minute RPO and one hour RTO only after the storage, database, and staffing model can prove it under test [23][25]. 1 2 The safest recovery plan is deliberately boring. It records what is backed up, where it is stored, which identity can read it, how often restore tests run, and which checks make the restore complete. A backup that cannot be selected, decrypted, restored, and verified by someone other than its author is an aspiration, not an operational control [15][23]. 1 2 Protect the layers separately: etcd snapshot for cluster object state, GitOps for declared desired state, Velero for Kubernetes resources, CSI or file system backup for volume data, and app native backup for databases with consistency requirements [1][11][14][18]. 1 2 Do not call a restore complete when the objects are visible; complete it only after readiness checks pass, service smoke tests succeed, telemetry is present, and the service owner accepts the chosen recovery point [8][19][20]. 1 2 Write explicit assumptions next to every RPO/RTO target: backup frequency, snapshot durability, restore location, object store availability, credentials, operator access, and validation commands [23][25]. 1 2 Decision Matrix No single Kubernetes backup mechanism is the correct answer for every incident. etcd restore is powerful but blunt: it is appropriate for control plane loss or catastrophic object store corruption, not for casually reverting one namespace after a bad deployment. Velero restore is better for resource level recovery and migration, but persistent data recovery depends on the volume protection mode and the storage driver underneath [1][2][12][13]. 1 2 The decision point is the smallest safe blast radius that restores the service objective. A deleted ConfigMap, failed rollout, or accidental namespace removal should normally be handled through GitOps reconciliation or a scoped Velero restore. A corrupted control plane with no trustworthy API state may require etcd snapshot restore. A corrupted database volume should usually prefer the database's own backup and consistency workflow unless the CSI snapshot process is known to produce a durable, application consistent recovery point [2][13][14][18]. 1 2 Control plane destroyed or etcd data directory lost: restore every etcd member from the same verified snapshot, create a new logical cluster, and use revision bump plus compaction when restoring an older snapshot into Kubernetes controller environments [1][2]. 1 2 Namespace or application resource accidentally deleted: restore from a Velero backup or let the GitOps controller reconcile from versioned desired state; prefer the narrower path that avoids overwriting unrelated live resources [12][13][18]. 1 2 PersistentVolume quick restore in the same environment: use CSI snapshots only after confirming the VolumeSnapshot CRDs, snapshot controller, snapshot class, and CSI driver capabilities are installed and tested [3][14]. 1 2 Cross cluster or cross provider restore: prefer Velero file system backup or application native backup when CSI snapshot portability or durability is not guaranteed by the driver and storage backend [13][14]. 1 2 GitOps controller loss: rebuild application manifests from Git, and restore controller state with the product's supported export/import flow when that state is not fully represented in repositories [17][18]. 1 2 Pre maintenance safety point: run velero backup create from schedule <schedule name and record the resulting backup name before risky upgrades; record an etcd snapshot status table for control plane work [1][12]. 1 2 Reference Architecture A reliable Kubernetes recovery architecture keeps the write paths independent. The Git repository is the versioned source for declarative desired state, the etcd snapshot store holds encrypted control plane recovery points, the Velero backup storage location holds resource backups and related metadata, and the volume layer holds either CSI snapshots, file system backups, or application native backups depending on workload requirements [1][11][14][18]. These stores should not share one untested credential path, one unmonitored lifecycle policy, or one undocumented retention rule. 1 2 The restore lane should be as real as production without being production. Maintain a small isolated cluster or namespace pattern where a selected backup can be restored, probes can run, and smoke tests can verify ingress, DNS, secrets, storage binding, and application behavior. The telemetry lane should survive or be quickly rebuilt after restore: Prometheus alerting rules, Alertmanager routing, and OpenTelemetry collection need enough context to distinguish a successful recovery from a quiet monitoring failure [19][20][21][22]. 1 2 Storage deserves a dedicated design review. StorageClasses define provisioners, parameters, reclaim policy, and binding mode, while PersistentVolumes have a lifecycle separate from Pods and PersistentVolumeClaims. That means restored manifests can look correct while volumes fail to bind, bind in the wrong topology, or inherit a reclaim policy that is dangerous during cleanup [4][5]. 1 2 Reference flow: Git repository GitOps controller Kubernetes API etcd; Velero schedule backup storage location; CSI snapshot or file system backup volume data store; alerting and telemetry independent incident channel [11][18][20][21]. 1 2 Keep backup credentials separate from routine deploy credentials where practical, and audit who can delete snapshots, object store backups, and backup schedules [15][23]. 1 2 Make storage driver caveats explicit: snapshot support, crash consistency, application consistency, topology constraints, cross region availability, deletion behavior, and whether snapshots remain durable after source volume deletion [3][14]. 1 2 Use StorageClass names and snapshot class names deliberately; a restored claim that points at a missing or incompatible class is a restore failure, not a Kubernetes mystery [3][4][5]. 1 2 Restore Runbook A restore begins by freezing the blast radius. Pause deployment automation, stop planned node drains, identify whether writes must be stopped at the application layer, and choose the recovery path before running destructive commands. The recovery lead should record the incident start time, selected RPO target, chosen backup name or snapshot file, namespaces in scope, storage classes in scope, and the validation owner for each service [13][15][25]. 1 2 Control plane restore and application restore should not be mixed casually. etcd restore reconstructs cluster state from a snapshot and can require every member to be rebuilt from the same point, with revision handling for Kubernetes controllers. Velero restore replays backed up Kubernetes objects and persistent volume data according to restore order, namespace mapping, resource policies, and the chosen volume restore mechanism [1][2][13]. 1 2 The runbook should end with proof, not optimism. A completed restore includes successful resource creation, bound volumes, healthy probes, service endpoints, application smoke tests, known data recovery point, and restored monitoring. If any admission webhook, quota, LoadBalancer, or storage binding issue blocks the process, capture logs and events before retrying so the next drill improves the runbook [8][15][26][27]. 1 2 Select recovery point: kubectl n velero get backups ; velero backup describe <backup name ; velero backup logs <backup name ; etcdutl write out=table snapshot status snapshot.db [1][12][13]. 1 2 Control plane template: ETCDCTL API=3 etcdctl endpoints "$ENDPOINT" snapshot save snapshot.db ; restore with etcdutl snapshot restore snapshot.db data dir <member data dir and apply revision bump and compaction options for older Kubernetes snapshots when required [1][2]. 1 2 Velero disaster template: set the BackupStorageLocation to read only before restoring, run velero restore create from backup <backup name , watch restore status and logs, then return the location to read write after validation [13][15]. 1 2 Namespace mapping template: velero restore create from backup <backup name namespace mappings old ns:new ns when testing a restore without overwriting the original namespace [13]. 1 2 GitOps controller template: after base infrastructure is healthy, restore Argo CD state with the supported argocd admin export and argocd admin import flow when controller state must be preserved beyond application manifests [17][18]. 1 2 Validation gate: check Pods, PVCs, Events, Services, ingress, readiness probes, synthetic transactions, alert state, and application data integrity before handing the service back [8][15][19][20]. 1 2 Failure Scenarios The most expensive restore failures are usually not command syntax errors; they are mismatched assumptions. A snapshot may exist but be tied to an unavailable storage backend, a restored object may be rejected by a namespace policy, an admission webhook may block its own dependency from returning, or a LoadBalancer service may not recreate the same external identity. Treat every restore drill as a search for these hidden assumptions [13][15][26][27]. 2 3 Stateful workloads introduce extra failure shapes. StatefulSets preserve identity and storage relationships, but deleting or scaling a StatefulSet does not automatically remove the associated volumes. That is good for data safety and dangerous for cleanup: stale claims and retained volumes can cause later restores to bind to the wrong data or fail because names are already taken [5][6]. 2 3 etcd quorum loss: stop further writes, preserve current data directories for forensics, restore all members from the same verified snapshot, and use new member data directories rather than mixing old and restored state [1][2]. 2 3 CSI snapshot not usable: confirm the VolumeSnapshot CRDs, snapshot controller, snapshot class, and driver support before assuming the snapshot is restorable or durable outside its original backend [3][14]. 2 3 Interrupted Velero backup: an InProgress backup that was interrupted should not be assumed resumable; create a fresh backup and inspect the diagnostic bundle and logs [15][16]. 2 3 Admission webhook blocks restore: temporarily disable or carefully sequence the webhook only when the restore target depends on resources the webhook prevents from being recreated; capture events first [16]. 2 3 LoadBalancer identity changes: expect provider specific service identifiers and DNS names to change after restore unless the provider and manifest explicitly preserve them; validate downstream DNS and allow lists [16]. 2 3 PDB prevents maintenance: a zero voluntary disruption budget can stop node drains; decide whether to change the budget, add replicas, or postpone maintenance rather than forcing disruption blindly [7]. 2 3 Backup verification CronJob overlaps: set concurrencyPolicy and startingDeadlineSeconds intentionally so delayed jobs do not stack up or silently skip after controller downtime [10]. 2 3 Quota or LimitRange rejection: inspect namespace ResourceQuota, LimitRange, Pod events, and restored requests/limits before blaming the scheduler or storage layer [26][27]. 2 3 Operational Checklist The checklist exists to keep recovery boring when people are tired. Daily checks should prove that scheduled backups completed and that alerts would fire when they do not. Weekly checks should sample restore metadata and storage driver assumptions. Monthly checks should perform an isolated restore of at least one stateless workload and one stateful workload. Quarterly checks should rehearse a full control plane or cluster level scenario that exercises credentials, DNS, ingress, secrets, and monitoring [11][12][15][19][20]. 1 2 The cadence should map to service tiers. A low criticality internal service can tolerate slower verification if its RTO/RPO is explicit. A regulated or revenue critical service needs tighter backup frequency, shorter evidence loops, and documented restoration sampling. NIST's contingency guidance connects backup frequency, alternate processing, confidentiality, integrity, and restoration sampling to recovery objectives; that is the right mental model for platform operations too [23][25]. 1 2 Daily: verify Velero schedule status, last backup completion time, failed backup alerts, object store reachability, and control plane snapshot job completion [11][12][15]. 1 2 Weekly: run velero backup describe <recent backup and inspect warnings, included namespaces, excluded resources, snapshot behavior, and retention policy [12][13]. 1 2 Monthly: restore one representative application into an isolated namespace using namespace mappings, verify PVC binding, probes, ingress, and application data checks [8][13]. 1 2 Quarterly: perform a control plane restore drill from an etcd snapshot into a disposable environment and verify revision handling, controller behavior, and GitOps reconciliation [1][2][18]. 1 2 Before upgrades: create an on demand backup from the normal schedule, capture an etcd snapshot status table, pause high risk automation, and confirm rollback owners [1][12]. 1 2 Telemetry: alert when backups are absent past the RPO window, when restore drills fail, when object store credentials expire, and when collector or Alertmanager outages would hide recovery signals [19][20][21][22]. 1 2 Common Pitfalls The most common pitfall is confusing a successful backup job with a recoverable system. A backup can finish while excluding the namespace that matters, skipping persistent data, storing snapshots in a backend that cannot be reached from the recovery cluster, or capturing resources that later fail admission because quotas and defaults changed. Good operators inspect restore behavior, not just backup status [12][13][15][26][27]. 3 4 Another common mistake is treating Kubernetes abstractions as application consistency guarantees. A VolumeSnapshot or file system backup may preserve blocks, but a database may still need its own flush, lock, checkpoint, or native backup process. StatefulSet identity helps workloads come back with stable names and storage relationships, but it does not decide whether the bytes are internally consistent [3][5][6][14]. 3 4 Do not rely on default StorageClass and reclaimPolicy behavior without documenting it; a Delete reclaim policy can be correct for ephemeral workloads and catastrophic for misunderstood restore cleanup [4][5]. 3 4 Do not assume CSI snapshots are portable or durable across clusters, regions, or providers unless the CSI driver and storage backend explicitly support that tested path [3][14]. 3 4 Do not let PDBs, rollout settings, or readiness probes drift out of sync with recovery assumptions; they shape maintenance safety and post restore traffic routing [7][8][9]. 3 4 Do not restore blindly over live namespaces; use namespace mappings or isolated clusters for drills and understand existing resource policy before touching production [13]. 3 4 Do not ignore Velero warnings about webhooks, LoadBalancer services, stuck backups, or missing credentials; those warnings often represent the exact issues that surface during an outage [15][16]. 3 4 Do not let backup CronJobs overlap silently; choose Allow , Forbid , or Replace based on what should happen when a previous verification job is still running [10]. 3 4 Maintenance Notes Backup documentation should have an owner, a review date, and evidence links to the last successful restore. Keep the runbook close to the commands, but keep sensitive values out of the wiki: endpoint names, credentials, encryption keys, and object store secrets belong in approved secret management systems, not in Markdown. The wiki should hold templates, assumptions, decision rules, and validation criteria [15][23]. 1 2 Review the page whenever Kubernetes, etcd, Velero, CSI drivers, cloud storage classes, ingress controllers, GitOps controllers, or monitoring stacks change. Recovery tooling is tightly coupled to versions and providers: a minor change to snapshot APIs, storage class defaults, or admission control can invalidate an otherwise polished runbook [2][3][4][13][14]. 1 2 Keep disaster exercises intentionally uncomfortable. A useful drill breaks a dependency, forces a human to select a recovery point, validates data quality, and records follow up work. The goal is not to prove the team is perfect; it is to reveal the next weak link before a real incident [24][25]. 1 2 Evidence to retain: backup name, snapshot identifier, object store location, selected recovery point, restore duration, data validation result, alerts observed, and follow up issues [12][13][19]. 1 2 Suggested maintenance cadence: daily backup health checks, monthly namespace restore, quarterly stateful restore, semiannual control plane restore, and annual RTO/RPO review with service owners [1][2][23][25]. 1 2 RPO example: a daily Velero backup may be acceptable for generated development workloads, but it is not acceptable for a service whose owners promised 15 minutes of maximum data loss [12][25]. 1 2 RTO example: a four hour RTO must include the time to obtain credentials, build or access the target cluster, restore resources, validate data, update DNS or traffic routing, and communicate service status [23][25]. 1 2 Version note: verify the active Kubernetes, etcd, Velero, CSI driver, and Argo CD documentation before changing commands in the live wiki; do not copy old syntax forward without a drill [1][2][13][17]. 1 2 Sources 1. kubernetes.io 2. etcd.io 3. kubernetes.io 4. kubernetes.io 5. kubernetes.io 6. kubernetes.io 7. kubernetes.io 8. kubernetes.io 9. kubernetes.io 10. kubernetes.io 11. velero.io 12. velero.io 13. velero.io 14. velero.io 15. velero.io 16. velero.io 17. argo cd.readthedocs.io 18. opengitops.dev 19. prometheus.io 20. prometheus.io 21. opentelemetry.io 22. opentelemetry.io 23. nvlpubs.nist.gov 24. sre.google 25. docs.cloud.google.com 26. kubernetes.io 27. kubernetes.io
